verdicts.note: Quote verbatim supports the nullified-if-not-required-for-progression principle and the Pokemon example, but does not contain the FarmVille crop-withering punishment condition or the Animal Crossing example; source inaccessible.

verdicts.claim: Roughly 20% of players who install a game churn within the first 2 minutes of the first launch, and only about half of first-session players return f
verdicts.note: Article states verbatim 'most games will lose 20% of installs within 2 minutes from first launch' and 'the typical return rate for subsequent first session play hovers around 50%'; 'steepest drop-off point' is a fair summary gloss of those figures.

verdicts.claim: The mean first session length across the analyzed games is 9 minutes, and 90% of games have a median first session under 20 minutes.
verdicts.note: Article states the mean First Session Time across all games is 9 minutes and 90% of games have a median first session length under 20 minutes.

verdicts.note: Article states games above the 9-minute mean average 31% Day 1 retention vs 20% below; the '11-point gap / ~10 minutes extra' is arithmetic/interpretive framing consistent with the text, not contradicted.

verdicts.note: Article states 'ensuring the typical first session is between 10-20 minutes is vital to have any chance of F2P success' and that content must be engaging, free from frustration, and leave players in a state conducive to returning.

verdicts.note: Article (by Mark Robinson, first published on the deltaDNA blog) states 'over 5,000 games in our deltaDNA platform', a 'sample of 275 comparable games', and data sets from October 2015 to February 2016.

verdicts.claim: Free-to-play mobile games typically lose about 20% of installs within the first two minutes of the first launch, making the immediate opening of the f
verdicts.note: Article states verbatim 'most games will lose 20% of installs within 2 minutes from first launch'.

verdicts.note: Article states the 9-minute mean and the 31% vs 20% Day 1 retention split exactly as claimed.

verdicts.note: Article states the 10-20 minute window is 'vital to have any chance of F2P success' and that 90% of games have a median first session under 20 minutes.

verdicts.note: Article prescribes engaging, frustration-free content for those 20 minutes and leaving the game in a state conducive to returning; 'session-end hooks' is a reasonable reading of that prescription.

verdicts.claim: Even among players who complete a reasonable first session, only about half come back for a second session, so first-session engagement alone does not
verdicts.note: Fair paraphrase of 'the typical return rate for subsequent first session play hovers around 50%'; the ~50% second-session return figure is verbatim in the text.

verdicts.note: Article contains the verbatim quote "We strive to make it not feel like a tutorial at all," and Fan's stated practice of never labeling sections as tutorials, teaching players 'without them ever realizing they're being taught.'

verdicts.claim: Fan claims players learn best by performing actions in-game rather than reading instructions, and that a single demonstration of an action's result is
verdicts.note: Article quotes Fan verbatim: "The best way for a player to learn is to actually perform actions in the game" and "Once they see the results of their action, that's often all it takes for them to understand that action."

verdicts.note: Article confirms mechanics were 'introduced very slowly' with money not coming into play until 'the player completes 10 levels'; caveat: the article says 'money' (framed as a peripheral mechanic), not 'sun', and does not call it a core currency.

verdicts.claim: Fan prescribes a strict limit on tutorial text to keep early-session cognitive load low: no more than eight words on screen at any given moment, deliv
verdicts.note: Article states verbatim 'a maximum of eight words on the screen at any given moment,' the 'eloquent caveman' style, and the boy-who-cried-wolf warning that bombarding players with irrelevant messages makes them tune out.

verdicts.claim: Fan advocates teaching through visual design and prior real-world knowledge instead of explicit instruction — e.g., Peashooters have prominent mouths
verdicts.note: Article confirms the Peashooter 'has a giant mouth for spitting projectiles' and 'Zombies... are known for moving slowly' (leveraging familiar concepts); the 'rooted plants' example specifically was not found in the text but the core teaching-via-design/prior-knowledge point is backed.

verdicts.note: Article contains the verbatim quote "I go out of my way to never call any sections of my game the tutorial… humans like learning and learning is inherently fun, but sometimes we have to trick them."

verdicts.claim: Fan prescribes a hard cap of roughly eight words of instructional text on screen at any given moment during onboarding, on the grounds that verbose in
verdicts.note: Article states 'There should be a maximum of eight words on the screen at any given moment' and that irrelevant message bombardment causes players to tune out.

verdicts.note: Article confirms conservative, slow introduction of mechanics with money delayed until the player completes 10 levels; caveat: the article says 'money' (a peripheral mechanic in its framing), not 'sun', and does not describe the delayed element as a core system.

verdicts.claim: Having the player perform an action once and see its result teaches a mechanic more effectively than written explanation — active 'doing' beats 'readi
verdicts.note: Article quotes Fan: "Once they see the results of their action, that's often all it takes for them to understand that action," and that performing actions in-game is the best way to learn.

verdicts.claim: Adaptive messaging — showing hints only to players who are demonstrably making mistakes — preserves competence feelings for skilled players, aligning
verdicts.note: Article describes Fan's system that 'would give tips to players who were doing the wrong thing, while other players wouldn't see any tips at all'; the SDT/competence framing is the claim author's gloss but the adaptive-messaging mechanism is backed.

verdicts.note: quote-consistent, source inaccessible — quote states the study examined science-fiction theme influence on comprehension and that theme preferences affect narrative understanding.

verdicts.note: quote-consistent, source inaccessible — quote states the design was efficacious in storytelling with feedback suggesting a highly engaging storied experience.

verdicts.note: quote-consistent, source inaccessible — quote matches the definition of clicking to accumulate currency with eventually less and even no interaction.

verdicts.note: quote-consistent, source inaccessible — quote describes iteratively developing the game from the framework and player feedback and testing the story-information complexity it could carry.

verdicts.claim: The appeal and well-being effects of video games derive from their capacity to satisfy the three basic psychological needs of self-determination theo
verdicts.note: Paper states verbatim that appeal and well-being effects are based in satisfying needs for competence, autonomy, and relatedness, and that 'the SDT model of need satisfaction predicts sustained engagement over time, as well as the short-term effects of game activities on players' well-being.'

verdicts.claim: In Ryan, Rigby & Przybylski's (2006) study of online game players, competence, autonomy, and relatedness need satisfaction each made independent cont
verdicts.note: The paper reports of the final Ryan et al. (2006) study (self-selecting players): 'all three needs made independent contributions to game enjoyment, immersion, and estimations of future engagement,' with only autonomy and competence accounting for unique incremental variance in postplay affect.

verdicts.claim: Intuitive mastery of a game's controls/interface is a necessary-but-not-sufficient gate for engagement: players must feel intuitive command of basic
verdicts.note: Text states mastery of controls 'constituted a necessary but not sufficient condition' (results supported this), and Przybylski (2009) studies show complex interfaces and lack of practice time frustrated competence and produced pre- to postplay aggression increases — 'it was an inability to master the game controls instead of differences in violent game content' that drove aggression shifts.

verdicts.claim: Violent content is not, on average, a significant motivator of play once need satisfaction is accounted for — autonomy and competence satisfaction, n
verdicts.note: Paper states verbatim 'violent game content is not, on average, a significant motivator of play,' that autonomy/competence satisfaction accounted for enjoyment, immersion, and preference for future play, and that these processes are 'robust predictors over and above differences in player demographics' applying 'across game genres and content.'

verdicts.claim: Players whose basic psychological needs are unmet in everyday life develop an 'obsessive passion' for games — a compulsive engagement pattern marked
verdicts.note: Paper reports low everyday need satisfaction predicted obsessive passion linked to 'a disordered pattern: less game enjoyment, negative postplay mood, and yet a higher overall amount of engagement,' while need-satisfied players showed harmonious, volitional engagement; the 'dark-pattern-exploitable' phrasing is the claimant's framing, not attributed to the paper.

verdicts.claim: Medium-term goals (the 'metagame') are the most important tier of a game's goal hierarchy for retention: they bridge moment-to-moment gameplay and long-
verdicts.note: Article states verbatim that from the retention point of view medium term goals 'arguably have the biggest importance', 'form the bridge between the moment to moment gameplay and the ultimate aspiration goal', 'form the backbone of the retention structure', occupy 'the bulk of the player's time', and are 'usually referred to as the metagame'.

verdicts.claim: Optimizing a game for player retention consists of structuring the player's time by shaping their aspirations across layered goal horizons, rather tha
verdicts.note: Article states verbatim: 'when we are optimizing a game for player retention, what we are doing is actually structuring the player's time, by influencing his aspirations', within a framework of three layered goal timeframes.

verdicts.claim: Games typically reveal goals to new players in a specific non-chronological order: the long-term aspirational goal first (to establish context), then
verdicts.note: Article verbatim: aspirational goal presented first, 'Next, the player is presented with the short term goal' via 'some sort of a tutorial', and 'Finally and only after the player has established at least the rudimentary level of skill, medium term goals are gradually revealed'.

verdicts.claim: Only about 10% of players ever finish AAA games on average, implying long-term goals function as aspirational framing rather than content most players
verdicts.note: Article states 'according to some estimates, even in the world of AAA games, on the average only about 10% of players ever reach the end of a game' and defines long-term goals as aspirational ones players are 'by no means guaranteed to reach'.

verdicts.claim: Short-term goals are defined as the moment-to-moment or minute-to-minute interaction layer (core mechanics), distinct from the metagame layer — a taxo
verdicts.note: Article defines short term goals as 'moment to moment, or minute to minute interaction... a domain of the core gameplay', while the metagame label is attached to medium-term goals.

verdicts.claim: Medium-term goals (the metagame) are the most important tier for player retention, forming the backbone of the retention structure and occupying most
verdicts.note: Matches verbatim page text: medium term goals 'arguably have the biggest importance' for retention, 'form the backbone of the retention structure', occupy 'the bulk of the player's time', and are 'usually referred to as the metagame'.

verdicts.claim: Games should present goals in a specific onboarding sequence: the aspirational long-term goal first (to establish context), then short-term goals duri
verdicts.note: Article describes exactly this sequence (aspirational goal first, short term goal via tutorial, medium term goals only after rudimentary skill); the article frames it descriptively ('Typically') rather than prescriptively, but all load-bearing elements are backed.

verdicts.claim: Optimizing a game for retention is fundamentally an exercise in structuring the player's time by shaping their aspirations across layered goal horizon
verdicts.note: Verbatim page text: 'when we are optimizing a game for player retention, what we are doing is actually structuring the player's time, by influencing his aspirations'.

verdicts.claim: Only about 10% of players ever finish AAA games on average, implying long-term/aspirational goals are not what actually retains most players and are n
verdicts.note: Article states the ~10% AAA completion estimate verbatim and defines long-term goals as aspirational goals players are 'by no means guaranteed to reach'.

verdicts.claim: Player goals can be usefully taxonomized into three tiers by time investment: short-term goals are the moment-to-moment core gameplay, medium-term goa
verdicts.note: Article divides goals into three timeframes exactly as claimed: short term as moment-to-moment core gameplay, medium term as the metagame/retention backbone, long term as aspirational goals not guaranteed to be reached.

verdicts.note: quote-consistent, source inaccessible (video-only page); visible session abstract verbatim confirms 'defined RimWorld not as a game, but as a story generator... opened up entirely new mechanisms for creating compelling play'.

verdicts.note: quote-consistent, source inaccessible; visible abstract confirms 'by strategically leaving out features, they made players engage with features and story elements that aren't actually there' — note the 'apophenia' label is the researcher's gloss, not in the abstract.

verdicts.note: quote-consistent, source inaccessible; abstract confirms 'hit Steam in summer 2016 with remarkable success... tiny team... genre littered with failures', though the abstract says only 'a genre' without naming it colony-sim (an uncontroversial external identification).

verdicts.note: quote-consistent, source inaccessible; abstract confirms 'how they made better decisions by not planning despite everyone wanting them to plan'.

verdicts.note: quote-consistent, source inaccessible — saved quote states path analyses showed in-game need frustration is a distinct construct predicting negative outcomes uniquely.

verdicts.note: Saved quote confirms survey data from online video gamers and path analysis but contains neither the load-bearing 'Amazon MTurk' recruitment nor 'cross-sectional' detail; source returned HTTP 403.

verdicts.note: quote-consistent, source inaccessible — saved quote states the findings suggest assessing both need satisfaction and frustration in gaming research could prove valuable.

verdicts.note: Description says engaging within first minutes 'has become a critical aspect of development in the era of free-to-play games'; the sunk-cost rationale in parentheses is the claimant's gloss, not stated on the page.

verdicts.note: Description states the talk will 'cover the common onboarding pitfalls, provide guidelines from User Experience (UX), and discuss best-practices by using knowledge of how the brain learns.'

verdicts.note: Description says the talk 'will provide examples from various titles (including insights from the development of Epic Games' Fortnite)'; note Fortnite was still in development (unshipped) in 2016, so 'shipped-title' is the claimant's slight overstatement, but the load-bearing Fortnite attribution is verbatim on the page.
